title: Lawrence Quizon
text:
Lawrence Quizon
      
      
        Mixed Signal Accelerators · Efficient AI · Researcher @ UP EEEI
      
      
        My primary research interest is in the creation of efficient computational systems in software and hardware for the advancement of both engineering 
        (low power computation on the edge, neuromorphic hardware) 
        and sciences 
        (device physics, emergent dynamics).
      
      
        
          
          GitHub
        
        
          
          LinkedIn
        
        
          
          Google Scholar
        
      
    
  





  
    
      Primary Research
      
        Efficient computational systems in software and hardware—mixed-signal accelerators, in-memory computing, and neural architecture search for edge deployment.
      
    
    
      Secondary Interests
      
        Open Source Hardware
        Hardware CI/CD
        Differential Equations
        Discrete Optimization
        Efficient AI
        Physics (GR, QFT)
        Abstract Math
        Learning Theory
      
    
  





  Recent Pages
  
    Notes below are sometimes attempts on active recall, sometimes just literal pastebins while working on something.

title: Transistor Capacitances
text:
General Frequency Response
Most amplifiers have a frequency response that looks like the following:

title: Transistor Capacitances
text:
Everything is a capacitor

If something holds charge, it is a capacitor.
\[C = \frac{dQ}{dV} \]


Hence, everywhere in semiconductor devices where there are holes and electrons moving in and out there is capacitance.

title: Transistor Capacitances
text:
BJT Parasitics


There are two main parasitic capacitances in a BJT:


Base-Emitter Capacitance \(C_{\pi} = C_b + C_{je}\) (Base Charging Capacitance + Base-Emitter Junction Capacitance)
Base-Collector Capacitance \(C_{\mu}\)

title: Transistor Capacitances
text:
The base charging capacitance \(C_b\)

Before we start using the BJT, there is effectively “no charge” in the base region.


However, as we turn the BJT on, the emitter starts to inject carriers (charge) into the base.

title: Transistor Capacitances
text:
The base charging capacitance \(C_b\)

The amount of carrier charge entering/exiting the base per second is \(I_{C}\)


If the amount of time a carrier spends in the base is \(\tau_F\), then the total charge stored in the base is: \[ Q_B =  I_C \tau_F \]


So then a change in \(V_{BE}\) can cause a change in \(Q_B\) through \(I_C\):



\[ C_b = \frac{dQ_B}{dV_{BE}} \]


\[ C_b = \frac{dQ_B}{dV_{BE}} = \frac{d(I_C \tau_F)}{dV_{BE}} \]


\[ C_b = \frac{dQ_B}{dV_{BE}} = \frac{d(I_C \tau_F)}{dV_{BE}} = \tau_F \frac{dI_C}{dV_{BE}} \]



Hence,
\[ C_b = \tau_F g_m \]

title: Transistor Capacitances
text:
The BE Junction Capacitance \(C_{je}\)


A depletion region is a region with charges.






As we discussed before, the width of the depletion region changes with applied voltage. It is known that \(W_{dep} \propto \sqrt{V_{bi} - V_{BE}}\).

title: Transistor Capacitances
text:
The BE Junction Capacitance \(C_{je}\)

Hence, since \(Q_{dep} \propto W_{dep}\), we have that some constant of proportionality \(\alpha\) exists such that:


\[ Q_{dep} = \alpha \sqrt{V_{bi} - V_{BE}} \]


\[ C_{je} = \left|\frac{dQ_{dep}}{dV_{BE}}\right| = \left|\alpha \frac{d(\sqrt{V_{bi} - V_{BE}})}{dV_{BE}}\right| = \left|-\frac{\alpha}{2\sqrt{V_{bi} - V_{BE}}}\right|  = \frac{\alpha}{2\sqrt{V_{bi}}\sqrt{1 - V_{BE}/V_{bi}}}\]


We can treat \(\frac{\alpha}{2\sqrt{V_{bi}}}\) as a constant \(C_{je0}\):
\[ C_{je} = \frac{C_{je0}}{\sqrt{1 - V_{BE}/V_{bi}}} = \frac{C_{je0}}{\sqrt{1 - V_{BE}/V_{j,BE}}} \]


and we see that \(C_{je0}\) is base-emitter junction capacitance measured at \(V_{BE} = 0\) V. We also typically refer to \(V_{bi}\) for the BE junction as \(V_{j,BE}\).

title: Transistor Capacitances
text:
The BE Junction Capacitance \(C_{je}\)
\[\frac{C_{je0}}{\sqrt{1 - V_{BE}/V_{j,BE}}}\]

title: Transistor Capacitances
text:
The BC Junction Capacitance \(C_{\mu}\)
Likewise, the BC junction’s depletion region also has a capacitance. We call this \(C_{\mu}\).
Since it’s usually reverse biased, we replace \(V_{BE}\) with \(V_{CB}\) and negate the sign in the denominator:
\[ C_{\mu} = \frac{C_{\mu0}}{\sqrt{1 + V_{CB}/V_{j,CB}}} \]







Note


We call it \(C_\mu\) and not \(C_{jc}\) because of its position in the small-signal equivalent. \(\mu\) is traditionally used to represent voltage feedback from output to input in the hybrid-pi model.

title: Transistor Capacitances
text:
BJT Parasitics Summary




\(C\mu\) - Junction capacitance between B and C
\(C_\pi = C_b + C_{je}\)

\(C_b\) - Capacitance from carriers crossing the base
\(C_{je}\) - Junction capacitance between B and E










Tip


\(C_\mu\) and is typically around 5fF-10fF.
\(C_{je}\) is a little higher, about \(>10fF\).
\(C_b\) is typically around hundreds of fF.

title: Transistor Capacitances
section: BJT Small Signal Model with Capacitances
text:
BJT Small Signal Model with Capacitances



\[C\pi=C_b+C_{je}\] \[C_b=\tau_Fg_m\]

\[\frac{C_{je0}}{\sqrt{1 - V_{BE}/V_{j,BE}}}\]

\[ C_{\mu} = \frac{C_{\mu0}}{\sqrt{1 + V_{CB}/V_{j,CB}}} \]

title: Transistor Capacitances
text:
MOS Parasitics


The MOS has three sources of parasitics:

Drain/Source to Bulk Junction Capacitance \(C_{db}\),\(C_{sb}\)  - Same as earlier.
Gate-bulk capacitance \(C_{gb}\) - The MOS Capacitor. Remember this?
Gate-overlap capacitance \(C_{gs}\)  - MOS Capacitor but we accidentally do it to S/D.

title: Transistor Capacitances
text: MOS Parasitics

title: Transistor Capacitances
text:
Overlap Capacitances \(C_{GS}\) and \(C_{GD}\)




\(C_{GS}\) and \(C_{GD}\) are overlap capacitances caused by an effective MOS capacitor on the overlap of the G region to the S and D regions.


\[C_{GS} = C_{GD} = \frac{\epsilon_{ox} x_d W}{t_{ox}}\]


But if you may remember…


\[C_{ox}=\frac{\epsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}}\]


\[C_{GS} = C_{GD} = C_{ox}x_dW\]

title: Transistor Capacitances
text:
The Gate-bulk Capacitance \(C_{gb}\)



As we discussed before, the main physical device behind the MOSFET is a MOS capacitor.


\[ C_{gb} = \frac{\epsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}}WL \]

title: Transistor Capacitances
text:
The Drain-bulk and Source-bulk Capacitances \(C_{db}\),\(C_{sb}\)
\(C_{db}\) and \(C_{sb}\) come from junction capacitances of the PN junction formed by the opposite-type semiconductors of the drain/source and the bulk.

title: Transistor Capacitances
text:
MOSFET Capacitances


MOS Parallel Plate Capacitances

\(C_{gs}\)
\(C_{gd}\)
\(C_{gb}\)

S/D-Bulk Junction Capacitances

\(C_{sb}\)
\(C_{db}\)

title: Transistor Capacitances
text:
Effective MOS Small-signal with Capacitances


The bulk is typically connected to the source meaning:



\(C_{gs}\) typically also accounts for \(C_{gb}\).




\(C_{sb}\) is shorted.



So, only \(C_{gs}\), \(C_{gd}\) and \(C_{db}\) survive.

title: Transistor Capacitances
text:
The fundamental gains
Let’s see what happens to the fundamental gains due to the capacitances:



Voltage gain:


\[ \frac{v_o}{r_o || 1/sC\mu} + v_i (g_m + sC\mu) = 0 \]


\[ \frac{v_o}{v_i} = - (g_m + sC\mu)(\frac{r_o}{1+sC\mu r_o}) \]


\[ \frac{v_o}{v_i} = - g_mr_o \frac{(1 + sC\mu/g_m)}{(1+sC\mu r_o)} \]






Current gain:


\[ i_o = v_i (g_m - sC\mu) \]


\[ i_i = v_i (sC\pi + sC\mu + 1/r_{\pi}) \]


\[ \frac{i_o}{i_i} = \frac{g_m-sC\mu}{1/r_{\pi} + sC_\pi+sC_\mu}\]


\[ \frac{i_o}{i_i} = g_mr_{\pi} \frac{1-sC\mu/g_m}{1+sr_{\pi}(C_\pi+C_\mu)}\]

title: Transistor Capacitances
text:
The Transition Frequency \(f_T\)



You’ll notice that the current gain reaches 1 (0 dB), but the voltage gain doesn’t.


Hence, people measure the “speed of the transistor” or the transition frequency as \[ \left| \frac{i_o}{i_i} \right|_{f_T} = 1\]

title: Transistor Capacitances
text:
The Transition Frequency \(f_T\)


\[ \frac{i_o}{i_i} = g_mr_\pi \frac{1-sC\mu/g_m}{s(C_\pi+C_\mu)}\]


\[ \frac{i_o}{i_i} = \frac{g_m-sC\mu}{1/r_\pi + s(C_\pi+C_\mu)}\]


\[ \left| \frac{i_o}{i_i} \right| = \frac{\sqrt{g_m^2 + \omega_T^2C_\mu^2}}{\sqrt{(1/r_\pi)^2 + \omega_T^2(C_\pi+C_\mu)^2}} = 1\]


\[ \frac{g_m^2 + \omega_T^2C_\mu^2}{(1/r_\pi)^2 + \omega_T^2(C_\pi+C_\mu)^2} = 1\]

title: Transistor Capacitances
text:
The Transition Frequency \(f_T\)


\[ \frac{g_m^2 + \omega_T^2C_\mu^2}{(1/r_\pi)^2 + \omega_T^2(C_\pi+C_\mu)^2} = 1\]


\[ \omega_T^2 = \frac{g_m^2 - (1/r_\pi)^2}{(C_\pi+C_\mu)^2 - C_\mu^2}\]


Since \(g_m^2 \gg 1/r_\pi^2\) and \((C_\pi+C_\mu)^2 \gg C_\mu^2\)






Definition


\[\omega_T \approx \frac{g_m}{C_\pi + C_\mu}, f_T \approx \frac{g_m}{2\pi(C_\pi + C_\mu)}\]

title: Transistor Capacitances
text:
The Transition Frequency \(f_T\)
For a MOSFET, everything is the same.
Set \(r_\pi \rightarrow \infty\)



Definition


\[\omega_T \approx \frac{g_m}{C_{gd} + C_{gs}}, f_T \approx \frac{g_m}{2\pi(C_{gd} + C_{gs})}\]

title: Transistor Capacitances
text:
The Transition Frequency \(f_T\)




Transistor Capacitances

title: Setting up an OS PDK
text:
Steps Overview
The following tools require setup for a new PDK:

Yosys Synthesis
OpenROAD (place_opt, global_route, detailed_route, cts)
MAGIC setup (sets up DRC)
NETGEN setup (LVS)
OpenSTA settings
OpenRCX settings
XSchem (Symbol creation and spice attachment)

PDK_HOME/libs.tech/xschem

title: Setting up an OS PDK
text:
Resources
OpenLane Discussions Issue on the original repo discussing PDK setup: https://github.com/The-OpenROAD-Project/OpenLane/discussions/2133
PDK Configuration Official documentation on setting up a configuration: https://openlane.readthedocs.io/en/latest/reference/pdk_configuration.html

title: Setting up an OS PDK
text:
Most important requirement: Config files
In fact, little else matters because these config files point to everything that’s needed. If they’re not there, you have to make them.

Required Files:

PDK Config: {pdk_root}/{pdk}/libs.tech/librelane/config.tcl

SCL Config: {pdk_root}/{pdk}/libs.tech/librelane/{scl}/config.tcl

title: Setting up an OS PDK
text:
What’s a TLEF?
You don’t actually need it! The configuration file is where you declare the tech lef.
# Technology LEF
set ::env(TECH_LEF) "$::env(PDK_ROOT)/$::env(PDK)/libs.ref/$::env(STD_CELL_LIBRARY)/techlef/$::env(STD_CELL_LIBRARY)__nom.tlef"
set ::env(TECH_LEF_MIN) "$::env(PDK_ROOT)/$::env(PDK)/libs.ref/$::env(STD_CELL_LIBRARY)/techlef/$::env(STD_CELL_LIBRARY)__min.tlef"
set ::env(TECH_LEF_MAX) "$::env(PDK_ROOT)/$::env(PDK)/libs.ref/$::env(STD_CELL_LIBRARY)/techlef/$::env(STD_CELL_LIBRARY)__max.tlef"
set ::env(CELLS_LEF) [glob "$::env(PDK_ROOT)/$::env(PDK)/libs.ref/$::env(STD_CELL_LIBRARY)/lef/*.lef"]
set ::env(GDS_FILES) [glob "$::env(PDK_ROOT)/$::env(PDK)/libs.ref/$::env(STD_CELL_LIBRARY)/gds/*.gds"]
set ::env(STD_CELL_LIBRARY_CDL) "$::env(PDK_ROOT)/$::env(PDK)/libs.ref/$::env(STD_CELL_LIBRARY)/cdl/$::env(STD_CELL_LIBRARY).cdl"

Note: Except for libs.ref (which is standard), everything else is handled by this config file.

title: Setting up an OS PDK
text:
Configuration Files Detail
Source: OpenLane Docs

config.tcl: Common info for all SCLs under this PDK. Example
common_pdn.tcl: PDN configuration.
<scl>/config.tcl: SCL specific info (overrides PDK config). Example
<scl>/tracks.info: Metal layer offsets and pitches (extracted from tech lef). Example

Format: <layer name> X|Y <offset> <pitch>

<scl>/synth_exclude.cells: Newline-separated cell names to trim during synthesis. Example
rcx_patterns.rules: Rules for OpenSTA. Example

title: Setting up an OS PDK
text:
Folder Structure


<pdk_name>

libs.tech

openlane

config.tcl
common_pdn.tcl (Default PDN script)
<standard cell library>

config.tcl
tracks.info
no_synth.cells
drc_exclude.cells



libs.ref

skyxxx_fd_xx_xx

lef (Can contain techlef declarations)
techlef (Doesn’t have to be a folder)
lib
…

title: Setting up an OS PDK
text:
XSchem as the virtuoso equivalent

Note: The most basic way to start is skipping XSchem and using ngspice directly.

Checklist:

Write libs.tech/xschem/xschemrc
Create symbols for relevant elements

Suggest starting with nfet and pfet to try a flow.
Investigate sky130 symbols (open with XSchem) for reference.

title: Setting up an OS PDK
section: XSchem Symbols (like the Cadence symbol view)
text:
XSchem Symbols (like the Cadence symbol view)


You must create your own symbols (using XSchem).
You need to set global attributes for XSchem symbols for them to be parsed into a spice netlist properly.
Global attribute example () is shown on the right (model name: nfet_1v8)

 1 G {}
 2 K {type=nmos
 3 lvs_format="@spiceprefix@name @pinlist sky130_fd_pr__@model L=@L W=@W nf=@nf m=@mult"
 4 format="@spiceprefix@name @pinlist sky130_fd_pr__@model L=@L W=@W
 5 + nf=@nf ad=@ad as=@as pd=@pd ps=@ps
 6 + nrd=@nrd nrs=@nrs sa=@sa sb=@sb sd=@sd
 7 + mult=@mult m=@mult"
 8 template="name=M1
 9 W=1
10 L=0.15
11 nf=1 
12 mult=1
13 ad=\\"expr('int((@nf + 1)/2) * @W / @nf * 0.29')\\"
14 pd=\\"expr('2*int((@nf + 1)/2) * (@W / @nf + 0.29)')\\"
15 as=\\"expr('int((@nf + 2)/2) * @W / @nf * 0.29')\\"
16 ps=\\"expr('2*int((@nf + 2)/2) * (@W / @nf + 0.29)')\\"
17 nrd=\\"expr('0.29 / @W ')\\" nrs=\\"expr('0.29 / @W ')\\"
18 sa=0 sb=0 sd=0
19 model=nfet_01v8
20 spiceprefix=X
21 "
22 drc="fet_drc \{@name\\\\\} \{@symname\\\\\} \{@model\\\\\} \{@W\\\\\} \{@L\\\\\} \{@nf\\\\\}"}

title: Setting up an OS PDK
text:
MAGIC setup checklist
Basically, all layout and signoff-related things (except maybe logic equivalence) are done by magic TCL scripts. Checklist:

Process the given stdcell lefs to create maglefs
Write sky130A.tcl
Write sky130A.tech
Write sky130A.magicrc
Write all the custom TCL, like fillgen.







Note


I strongly recommend just copying then changing the fill and DRC scripts from the existing sky130/libs.tech/magic or ihp-sg13g2/libs.tech/magic






Setting up an OS PDK

title: Lawrence Roman A. Quizon
text:
DRAM failure rates (and most other semiconductor failure rates) follow an Arrhenius law (looking like the Neel-Brown Relaxation for magnetics) \[\underbrace{\lambda(T)}_{\text{failure rate}}=\lambda_0e^{-\frac{E_a}{k_BT}}\] \(E_a\) captures all the relevant failure parameters.
If failures are poisson-like, the survival rate is \[P(t)=e^{-\lambda t}\] from which we can get a mean retention time. \[\tau(T)=\frac{1}{\lambda(T)}=\tau_0e^{\frac{E_a}{k_BT}}\] So then if we want the probability of failure before refresh \(\leq \epsilon\) then we integrate the above to get \(\epsilon\) OR:
Based of the probability of NOT surviving before refresh time \(t_r\): \[P_{fail}(t_r)=1-e^{-\lambda t_r}\leq \epsilon\] \[t_r \leq -\frac{1}{\lambda}\ln(1-\epsilon)\] then replace \(\lambda\) we get \[t_r(T)\leq \tau_0e^{-\frac{E_a}{k_BT}}\ln(\frac{1}{1-\epsilon})\] if \(\epsilon \ll 1\) then via linear approx
\[t_r(T)\lt \tau(T)\cdot \epsilon\]
When it’s field assisted with a field \(E\):
\[\lambda(T,E)\approx e^{-\frac{E_a-\alpha\sqrt E}{k_BT}}\] Practical form:
\[t_{ret}(T)=t_{ret}(T_0)\cdot e^{\frac{E_a}{k_B}(1/T-1/T_0)}\] This model is filled by:

Measure retention rate at some base temperature \(t_{ret}(T_0)\)
Characterize \(E_a/k_B\) experimentally by how the retention rate scales away from the \(T_0\) point, fitting against the Arrhenius model.

However, \(E_a\) is a probabilistic statistical variation (weaker cells exist, and cell strengths vary over time non-monotonically). So, we account for this by integrating the probabilities:
\[\lambda(T)=\int p(E_a) \lambda_0 e^{-E_a/k_BT}dE_a\] to find the probability distribution of the retention time.
This retention time is a parameter of the Poisson process. Observing failures gives a posterior over \(E_a\) but we start with an assumed \(E_a\).
The survival probability of cell \(i\) over refresh \(t_r\) is
\[P^{(i)}_{survival}=(t_r | E_{a,i})=e^{-t_r/\tau(E_{a,i},T)}\] based on the posterior \(E_a\) distribution.
Then, number of failures in a refresh interval is bernoulli with
\[p_i=e^{-t_r/\tau(E_{a,i},T)}\]
and the probability that ECC cannot correct this is the probability of losing more than \(k\) bernoulli trials:
\[Pr(X>k)\] Then for large \(N\) (trials = ncells) and small \(p_i\) (probability of failure) then
\[X~\text{ Poisson}(\lambda_{eff}),\lambda_{eff}=N\cdot(\bar p)\]
if \(\bar p\) is the average retention probability over the bernoulli distribution
\[\bar p=\mathbb{E}[1-e^{-t_r/\tau(E_a,T)}]\] Then so
\[P_{fail}(t_r,T)\approx1-\sum_j^K\frac{\lambda_{eff}^j e^{-\lambda_{eff}}}{j!}=1-\sum_j^K\frac{N\bar p e^{-N\bar p}}{j!}\] Then the probability that the data is at least recoverable, \[\sum_j^K\frac{N\bar p e^{-N\bar p}}{j!}\ge 1-\epsilon \] and we solve this over K bernoulli trials analytically or numerically versus retention time (which decides \(\bar p\)).
As a designer, we can infer behavior with first order approximations when \(\bar p \ll 1\) : \[\bar p \approx \frac{1}{N} (k+1)\epsilon\] then so
\[t_r(T)=\text{ solve: } \int p(E_a)[1-e^{-t_r/\tau(E_a,T)}]dE_a=\frac{(k+1)\epsilon}{N}\]

title: Lawrence Roman A. Quizon
text:
DRAM failure rates (and most other semiconductor failure rates) follow an Arrhenius law (looking like the Neel-Brown Relaxation for magnetics) \[\underbrace{\lambda(T)}_{\text{failure rate}}=\lambda_0e^{-\frac{E_a}{k_BT}}\] \(E_a\) captures all the relevant failure parameters.
If failures are poisson-like, the survival rate is \[P(t)=e^{-\lambda t}\] from which we can get a mean retention time. \[\tau(T)=\frac{1}{\lambda(T)}=\tau_0e^{\frac{E_a}{k_BT}}\] So then if we want the probability of failure before refresh \(\leq \epsilon\) then we integrate the above to get \(\epsilon\) OR:
Based of the probability of NOT surviving before refresh time \(t_r\): \[P_{fail}(t_r)=1-e^{-\lambda t_r}\leq \epsilon\] \[t_r \leq -\frac{1}{\lambda}\ln(1-\epsilon)\] then replace \(\lambda\) we get \[t_r(T)\leq \tau_0e^{-\frac{E_a}{k_BT}}\ln(\frac{1}{1-\epsilon})\] if \(\epsilon \ll 1\) then via linear approx
\[t_r(T)\lt \tau(T)\cdot \epsilon\]
When it’s field assisted with a field \(E\):
\[\lambda(T,E)\approx e^{-\frac{E_a-\alpha\sqrt E}{k_BT}}\] Practical form:
\[t_{ret}(T)=t_{ret}(T_0)\cdot e^{\frac{E_a}{k_B}(1/T-1/T_0)}\] This model is filled by:

Measure retention rate at some base temperature \(t_{ret}(T_0)\)
Characterize \(E_a/k_B\) experimentally by how the retention rate scales away from the \(T_0\) point, fitting against the Arrhenius model.

However, \(E_a\) is a probabilistic statistical variation (weaker cells exist, and cell strengths vary over time non-monotonically). So, we account for this by integrating the probabilities:
\[\lambda(T)=\int p(E_a) \lambda_0 e^{-E_a/k_BT}dE_a\] to find the probability distribution of the retention time.
This retention time is a parameter of the Poisson process. Observing failures gives a posterior over \(E_a\) but we start with an assumed \(E_a\).
The survival probability of cell \(i\) over refresh \(t_r\) is
\[P^{(i)}_{survival}=(t_r | E_{a,i})=e^{-t_r/\tau(E_{a,i},T)}\] based on the posterior \(E_a\) distribution.
Then, number of failures in a refresh interval is bernoulli with
\[p_i=e^{-t_r/\tau(E_{a,i},T)}\]
and the probability that ECC cannot correct this is the probability of losing more than \(k\) bernoulli trials:
\[Pr(X>k)\] Then for large \(N\) (trials = ncells) and small \(p_i\) (probability of failure) then
\[X~\text{ Poisson}(\lambda_{eff}),\lambda_{eff}=N\cdot(\bar p)\]
if \(\bar p\) is the average retention probability over the bernoulli distribution
\[\bar p=\mathbb{E}[1-e^{-t_r/\tau(E_a,T)}]\] Then so
\[P_{fail}(t_r,T)\approx1-\sum_j^K\frac{\lambda_{eff}^j e^{-\lambda_{eff}}}{j!}=1-\sum_j^K\frac{N\bar p e^{-N\bar p}}{j!}\] Then the probability that the data is at least recoverable, \[\sum_j^K\frac{N\bar p e^{-N\bar p}}{j!}\ge 1-\epsilon \] and we solve this over K bernoulli trials analytically or numerically versus retention time (which decides \(\bar p\)).
As a designer, we can infer behavior with first order approximations when \(\bar p \ll 1\) : \[\bar p \approx \frac{1}{N} (k+1)\epsilon\] then so
\[t_r(T)=\text{ solve: } \int p(E_a)[1-e^{-t_r/\tau(E_a,T)}]dE_a=\frac{(k+1)\epsilon}{N}\]

title: Lawrence Roman A. Quizon
text:
DRAM Refresh Troubles
DRAM refresh typically every \(64ms\) This occupies the DRAM row about \(500us\). A refresh command is given to a rank (like a word, 64b or 32b). A new refresh command is issued about every \(7.8us\) per rank (set of banks).
With precise compute control over the DRAM, we can manually control the refresh cycles that take about (\(~400n*\underbrace{8192}_{depth}=3.2ms\)) everytime (if done one after the other).

objectID: notes/Online Caching of Devcontainer builds with GHCR.io.html
href: notes/Online Caching of Devcontainer builds with GHCR.io.html
title: Online Caching of Devcontainer builds with GHCR.io
text:
Problem : Building and rebuilding docker images takes centuries. Solution : Cache build in GHCR.io

I wasn’t familiar with this yet when I started heavily using docker containers half a year ago (I think just half a year ago).
Devcontainers + Dockerfiles create reproducible and containerized development environments for every project.
However, if we’d like to stop working on a project, we want to delete these containers. When we want to open it again, we have to rebuild it locally.
To avoid this, we have to use the cachedFrom: option in the devcontainer.json:
{
  "name": "My Dev Environment",
  "build": {
    "dockerfile": "Dockerfile",
    "cacheFrom": [
      "ghcr.io/YOUR_GITHUB_USER/YOUR_REPO/devcontainer:latest"
    ]
  },
  "customizations": {
    // ... your vscode extensions, etc.
  }
}
Problem is, how does the build get pushed to the remote repository? In comes CI workflows.
You add a CI workflow that builds the image and pushes to the remote repository.
name: Build & push devcontainer image

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
    paths:
      - ".devcontainer/Dockerfile"
      - "environment.yml"

env:
  IMAGE: ghcr.io/lawrence-lugs/reponame[/something]

jobs:
  build-and-push:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    permissions:
      contents: read
      packages: write

    steps:
      - name: Checkout (GH)
        uses: actions/checkout@v4


      - name: Log in to GHCR
        uses: docker/login-action@v3
        with:
          registry: ghcr.io
          username: ${{ github.actor }}
          password: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}

      - name: Set up Docker Buildx
        uses: docker/setup-buildx-action@v3

      - name: Build and push
        uses: docker/build-push-action@v6
        with:
          context: .
          file: .devcontainer/Dockerfile
          push: true
          tags: ${{ env.IMAGE }}:latest
          cache-from: type=registry,ref=${{ env.IMAGE }}:buildcache
          cache-to: type=registry,ref=${{ env.IMAGE }}:buildcache,mode=max
In that case, the actions worker will build the image by itself and push it to the repository. Then, once the image is created, you can just pull it.

title: General Relativity
text:
Proper Time
Proper time is the time a moving particle experiences, because it’s defined as \[d\tau^2=-ds^2=-d\vec{x}\cdot d\vec{x}\] which has a consequence such that \[\frac{d\vec{x}}{d\tau}=(\vec{e_0})_{MCRF}\] Which is the time-basis of the “tangent reference frame” to the particle.


What is acceleration?
From the twins paradox, we know that both the stationary twin and the moving twin will observe that each other has a slower clock. However, when the moving twin “turns back” to travel towards the stationary twin, the stationary twin’s clock will have seemed like it ticked a lot instantly.
This means that the act of accelerating makes every other clock seem instantly faster.
Is this explainable by pure special relativity without curvature?
The twins paradox is not simply answered in “First Course in General Relativity Ch. 1.11”


Tensors
The one form: \[f_\alpha:=\mathbf{f}(\vec{e_\alpha})\] The two-form: \[f_{\alpha\beta}:=\mathbf{f}(\vec{e_\alpha},\vec{e_\beta})\] \[
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{f}(\vec{A},\vec{B})=\mathbf{f}(A^\alpha\vec{e_\alpha},B^\beta\vec{e_\beta})\\
=A^\alpha B^\beta \mathbf{f} ( \vec{e_\alpha},\vec{e_\beta} ) \\
=A^\alpha B^\beta f_{\alpha\beta}
\end{aligned}\] That is, all forms are just functions of increasing numbers of basis sets because they work on N vectors (for the N-form).
Just like a matrix, the two-form tensor is a sum over simple outer-products.


The Energy-Momentum 4-vector
This is called so because the definition of 4-momentum \[\vec{p}=m\vec{U}\] and since the 4-velocity \(\vec{U}\) is just \[\vec{U}=\{1,v_x,v_y,v_z\}\] this immediately leads to the discovery that its components \[p_\alpha=\{E,p_x,p_y,p_z\}\] are energy and the 3 spatial momenta.
In non-relativistic units, the first time would be \(E/c\).
This is because the time-component of \(\vec U\) is \(c\) in special relativity- which is further because \(ct\) is the unit of the time axis that we use.


Stress-Energy Tensor
\[T_{\alpha\beta}\] Its \(00\) is energy, \(0i\) and \(i0\) are symmetric and describe momentum and energy flux, which is just momentum. \(ij\) are momentum flux.
In fluids without heat transfer or viscosity (ideal space dust), the stress-energy tensor receives a \(0\) in momentum flux unless \(i=j\) where we are describing energy transfer via momentum through a surface.
Then, in such ideal fluids, stress-energy becomes:
\[T_{\alpha \beta}=(\rho+p)U^\mu U^\nu + pg^{\mu\nu}\]
The form above is for a ideal fluid. Which, technically at large scale, anything in the universe is. (Except, we have to account for viscosity sometime.)


Metric
The metric can usually be written as the formula for an arbitrary curve’s arclength. For example, in 2D polar coordinates: \[ds^2=r^2d\theta^2+dr^2\] We find the following insights:

The basis are not necessarily unit bases
The bases are not constant everywhere.



Christoffel Symbols
When we tried to differentiate a vector, we
We define the regular derivative (the derivative of each component of a vector): \[V^\alpha_{,\beta}=\frac{dV^\alpha}{dx^\beta}\vec{e^\alpha}\] This is the vector derivative in Cartesian Coordinates.
We can differentiate an arbitrary vector in arbitrary coordinates, but we found that some sort of product rule applies: \[\frac{d\vec{V}}{dx^\beta}=\frac{dV^\alpha}{dx^\beta}\vec{e^\alpha}+V^\alpha \frac{d\vec{e^\alpha}}{dx^\beta}\] We call this the covariant derivative.
In this equation, we use christoffel symbols instead to describe the change in basis against a change in the coordinates. \[V{^\alpha_{;\beta}} =V^\alpha_{,\beta}+\Gamma^\alpha_{\mu \beta} V^\mu\] Where, if the coordinates are not Cartesian, the Christoffel symbols \(\Gamma\) pop out to describe the shifting of the basis with respect to the origin.
They are a tensor describing how the basis changes with respect to coordinate change. \[\Gamma^\mu_{\alpha \beta}\vec{e_\mu}=\frac{d}{dx^\beta}\vec{e^\alpha}\]
Usefully, the Christoffel symbols can be calculated from the metric via:
\[\Gamma^\mu_{\alpha \beta}=\frac{1}{2}g^{\mu\nu}(g_{\nu \beta,\alpha}+g_{\alpha \nu,\beta}-g_{\alpha \beta,\nu})\]


Divergence in a locally flat euclidean space (g of signature +2)
The covariant derivative \[V{^\alpha_{;\beta}} =V^\alpha_{,\beta}+\Gamma^\alpha_{\mu \beta} V^\mu\] Simplifies to
\[V^\alpha_{;\alpha}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{-g}}(\sqrt{-g}V^\alpha)_{,\alpha}\]
If:

\(g_{\alpha \beta}\) is symmetric (which it is…)
???



Metric Volume/Length Element
It would make sense that the volume element in a manifold after coordinate transform would be: \[dx^{4'}=\det(g_{\alpha \beta})dx^4\] We typically refer to this determinant as \(g\)
The length of a curve is measured via:
\[\int_{\lambda_0}^{\lambda_1}|\vec{V}\cdot\vec{V}|d\lambda\] if \(\lambda\) is the parameter of the curve and \(\vec{V}\) is the vector tangent to the curve.
When we say \(\lambda\) is the parameter of the curve, the curve is defined as:
\[:\{\xi=f(\lambda),\eta=g(\lambda),\lambda_0\leq\lambda\leq\lambda_1\}\]
In differential geometry, we find a unique not-necessarily-unit tangent vector to any curve via: \[\frac{d\phi}{d\lambda}=<\tilde{d\phi},\vec{V}>\] and hence
\[\vec{V}=\left\{ \frac{d\xi}{d\lambda},\frac{d\eta}{d\lambda} \right\} \]


Geodesics
In a differentiable manifold that may be curved, the definition of a “line that always stays in the same direction” is in doubt.
As a proxy, the concept of enforcing parallel transport of the tangent vector is introduced. That is,
\[\nabla_{\vec{U}}\vec{V}=0\] where this notation means we take the derivative along the world line \[U^\beta V^\alpha_{;\beta}\] Iff \(\vec{U}\) is tangent to a geodesic \[\nabla_{\vec U}\vec{U}=0\] Then if \(U^\alpha=dx^\alpha/d\lambda\) and \(U^\beta \partial/\partial x^\beta=d/d\lambda\) then this condition leads to something that looks like the euler-lagrange equation only this time with a Christoffel symbol.
\[\frac{d}{d\lambda}\left( \frac{dx^\alpha}{d\lambda} \right) + \Gamma^\alpha_{\mu \beta}\frac{dx^\mu}{d\lambda}\frac{dx^\beta}{d\lambda}=0\]


Riemann Tensor
We can “measure” the curvature of a manifold by measuring how much the direction of the parallel transport changes over a closed loop.
This would give us a change in \(V^\alpha\) due to transport around the loop. \[\delta V^\alpha=\delta a \delta b \left[ {
\Gamma^\alpha_{\mu\sigma,\lambda} -
\Gamma^\alpha_{\mu\lambda,\sigma} -
\Gamma^\alpha_{\nu\lambda} \Gamma^\nu_{\mu\sigma} -
\Gamma^\alpha_{\nu\sigma} \Gamma^\nu_{\mu\lambda}
} \right]
V^\mu
\] Since \(V^\mu\) is a vector, we can think that the entire left side is the basis one-form that gives \(\delta V^\alpha\) from the vector \(\delta \vec{V}\).
\[R^\alpha_{\beta\mu\nu}=\text{set of christoffel terms}\] \[R^\alpha_{\mu \lambda \sigma} = {
\Gamma^\alpha_{\mu\sigma,\lambda} -
\Gamma^\alpha_{\mu\lambda,\sigma} -
\Gamma^\alpha_{\nu\lambda} \Gamma^\nu_{\mu\sigma} -
\Gamma^\alpha_{\nu\sigma} \Gamma^\nu_{\mu\lambda}
}\] We call this the Riemann tensor.
With the form of the Riemann tensor, we can see the following:

It has 3 lower (one-form) indices and 1 upper index.

3 lower indices means that if we give it 3 vectors, it becomes a vector.
The 3 vectors are \(V^\mu\), \(\delta a \vec{e_\mu}\), \(\delta b \vec{e_\nu}\).
In the end, we see that the Riemann curvature takes the infinitesimal area and a vector (any) as argument and gives a vector pointing out the curvature.
In a locally inertial frame \(P\): \[R^\alpha_{\beta \mu \nu} = \frac{1}{2}g^{\sigma \sigma}(g_{\sigma \nu, \beta \mu}-g_{\sigma \nu, \beta \nu} + g_{\beta \mu, \sigma \nu} - g_{\beta \nu, \sigma \mu})\]


Ricci Tensor
This identity is verifiable
\[R_{\alpha \beta \mu \nu}=-R_{\beta \alpha \mu \nu}\] and so if two of the indices are the same, then \(A=-A=0\) \[R_{\alpha\alpha\mu\nu}=0\] It’s also verifiable that all other index repetitions would result in the Riemann tensor disappearing to 0. EXCEPT THE RICCI TENSOR.
The Ricci tensor is ONLY nonzero contraction of the curvature tensor on the first upstairs and second downstairs index:
\[R_{\alpha \beta} = R^\mu_{\sigma \mu \beta}\] Applying the Ricci contraction,
\[R_{\mu\nu}=g^{\alpha\beta}R_{\alpha \mu \beta \nu}\]
and then further contracting gives the Ricci scalar:
\[R=g^{\mu\nu}R_{\mu\nu}\]
The Ricci scalar is the double trace of the Ricci tensor.


Einstein Tensor and Field Equation
Contracting the Bianchi Identities twice with the Ricci contraction turns the bianchi identities into:
\[R_{;\lambda}-R^\mu_{\lambda;\mu}-R^\mu_{\lambda;\mu}=0\] (Turning the Ricci scalar contravariant derivative into an identity matrix reduction…) \[(2R^\mu_\lambda-\delta^\mu_\lambda R)_{;\mu}=0\] (divide this by 2 and we get…) and the Einstein Tensor can be defined to be
\[G^{\alpha\beta} \equiv R^{\alpha \beta}-\frac{1}{2}g^{\alpha \beta}R=G^{\beta\alpha}\] because the metric tensor in (1 1) form is the identity, so if we raise everything we can get the metric tensor back.
The last one gives us \[G^{\alpha \beta}_{;\beta}=0\] Einsten would later show that this final tensor which is derived from the more pure Riemann curvature is the one that mathematically causes gravity: \[G^{\alpha \beta}=8\pi T^{\alpha \beta}\] because it is related to the stress-energy tensor- which itself encodes the total energy-mass content in space.
How do we get back useful computations from the einstein tensor?

Compute metric from einstein tensors
Compute geodesics from metric
done.



Computing the Apparent Horizon
The apparent horizon is the outermost marginally outer trapped surface. Here, \(\Theta\) (expansion of a congruence of outgoing null geodesics) \(=0\).

That is, at this timeslice, the photons never point outward.

We can express the apparent horizon as a parametric surface \(r(\theta,\phi)\).
By spectral composition using spherical harmonics, \[r(\theta,\phi)=\sum_{l=0}^L \sum_{m=-l}^{l} a_{lm}Y_{lm}(\theta,\phi)\] > In spherical harmonics, \(m\) can only range from \(-l\) to \(l\)
Then, the problem becomes finding \(a_{lm}\) coefficients.
So then, we need to find that \(a_{lm}\) provides a surface where \(\Theta=0\).
\[\Theta=s^i_{;i}+K_{ij}s^is^j-K=0\] We use iterative solvers to enforce that this is 0.
\[a_{lm}^{(new)}=a_{lm}^{(old)}-\delta a_{lm}\]


Computing the event horizon
Unlike the apparent horizon, the event horizon is defined globally. It is the boundary of the causal past of future null infinity.

The difference is that in the apparent horizon,



Readings

MASSIVE: 3+1 Formulation
Misner Ch. 12 “Variational Principle and Initial-Value Data”
Smarr “sources of gravitational radiation”
